articles.title: Jamie Katcher of JLL: 5 Questions
articles.narrative: JLL's Tech Practice Group, led by Jamie Katcher, was formed in 2025 to support clients navigating the rapid growth of artificial intelligence companies in New York City. AI companies significantly increased their leasing activity in Q1 2026, nearly matching their entire 2025 footprint. This surge is tightening the market for high-quality office assets, particularly in Midtown South, as AI firms prioritize premium spaces for recruitment and client engagement. Katcher notes that this wave feels more grounded than previous tech booms, driven by well-capitalized companies adapting to AI, and anticipates a 'musical chairs' effect as demand for top-tier buildings intensifies.

articles.title: New York’s Renewed Fight Against Property Deed Theft Confronts Key Challenges
articles.narrative: Deed theft, formally illegal in New York state only since 2023, remains a persistent and growing problem for single-family homeowners in New York City, exacerbated by the hot housing market and difficulties in prosecution. Despite new laws in 2023 and 2024 aimed at curbing the trend, complaints surged to 517 in 2025, up from 149 in 2023. Experts note that fraudsters continually adapt to new regulations, making it a challenging issue to resolve. The lack of public transparency regarding beneficial owners of shell corporations, despite the LLC Transparency Act, further complicates efforts to combat these scams. Recent legislative efforts have focused on pausing evictions and foreclosures related to fraudulent deeds, voiding such deeds, and extending the statute of limitations for criminal charges.

articles.title: Sunday Summary: Goodbye, Gotham? (Let’s Hope Not)
articles.market: New York City, NY / South Florida, FL
articles.narrative: This article is a weekly summary of significant commercial real estate news, primarily focusing on contrasting development and transaction activity in New York City and South Florida. It highlights a potential shift in investment sentiment by Ken Griffin of Citadel, who is reportedly reconsidering a major office development in New York while proceeding with a pure office play in Miami. The piece also covers several large industrial and residential transactions in South Florida, alongside notable office leases and a residential development joint venture in New York City, suggesting continued, albeit competitive, market activity in both regions.

articles.title: To succeed in today’s Twin Cities office sector? Landlords must work hard
articles.narrative: Newmark's Q1 2026 report on the Minneapolis-St. Paul office market indicates a challenging but potentially stabilizing environment. While overall vacancy slightly dipped to 19.9% and net absorption turned positive, much of this improvement is attributed to a shrinking inventory as obsolete office space is removed through conversions and demolitions. Leasing activity remains subdued, characterized by a "flight to quality" where tenants right-size their footprints into newer, amenity-rich buildings, creating a sharp divide between Class A and Class B properties. Landlords are responding by holding asking rents steady but increasing concessions, particularly in the struggling downtown cores where vacancy rates are significantly higher. Despite these challenges, the report highlights opportunities for opportunistic investors willing to acquire discounted assets and reposition them, supported by the region's diverse economy and educated workforce.
articles.market_intelligence: Overall office vacancy across the Twin Cities dipped to 19.9% in Q1 2026, down from 20.8% at the end of 2025. Office net absorption in the Minneapolis-St. Paul market turned positive, reaching nearly 50,000 square feet during the first quarter. Since 2024, more than 3.4 million square feet of office space has been taken out of the Twin Cities office inventory. In downtown Minneapolis, vacancy hovered just above 30% in Q1 2026, while St. Paul’s downtown office market posted a vacancy rate of about 37%. The region’s unemployment rate stood at 3.8% at the end of 2025.

articles.title: JLL Capital Markets brokers sale of five-property retail portfolio in Dallas, Fort Worth markets
articles.narrative: JLL Capital Markets brokered the sale of the N3 DFW Strip, a five-property retail portfolio located across the Dallas-Fort Worth metroplex, to Curbline Properties. The portfolio includes retail strip centers in Flower Mound, Hudson Oaks, Southlake, Red Oak, and Waco. The properties are strategically positioned in rapidly growing suburban markets, offering strong connectivity and favorable demographic trends, which are crucial for retail success. The transaction generated intense competitive bidding from institutional capital due to the limited availability of unanchored strip center portfolios at scale.

data_points.notable_features: Five retail strip centers strategically positioned in high-growth suburban markets with strong connectivity and favorable demographics.

articles.market_intelligence: The Dallas-Fort Worth metroplex is experiencing robust economic growth, with expanding populations and strong consumer spending supporting retail fundamentals. The region's diversified economy, major transportation infrastructure, and favorable business climate make it a highly sought-after market for retail investment.

articles.title: BOMA of Greater Minneapolis elects board chair, directors
articles.narrative: The Building Owners and Managers Association (BOMA) of Greater Minneapolis has elected new leadership, with Raymond Vaughn of Ryan Companies named Board Chair. Additionally, Xue Yang of Hempel, Kellee Vinge of Capital Partners, and Umberto Rivera of Transwestern were elected to the Board of Directors. These new leaders are expected to bring diverse perspectives and experience to further BOMA's mission of providing education, networking, and advocacy for commercial real estate professionals.

articles.title: JLL Capital Markets closes sale of 1.74-acre redevelopment site in Dallas
articles.narrative: JLL Capital Markets has facilitated the sale of Monticello West, a 1.74-acre redevelopment site in Dallas, TX, to McKinney Webb Holdings, LLC. The property, sold by Lakemore LCS Leasing MW LLC, is strategically located between Highland Park and the Knox-Henderson Avenue corridor, offering significant development potential for multifamily, office, or hotel uses due to its flexible zoning and proximity to amenities like the Katy Trail.

data_points.notable_features: Zoned PD 193 LC allowing for multifamily, office, or hotel development with a maximum height of 240 feet and a 4.5:1 FAR. Located between Highland Park and Knox-Henderson Avenue corridor, 300 feet from the Katy Trail.

articles.title: BKV Group completes work on 290-unit rental high-rise in downtown San Antonio
articles.narrative: BKV Group has completed The Continental Residences, a 290-unit rental high-rise developed by Weston Urban in downtown San Antonio. The 16-story building, located on the 1.9-acre Continental Block, features a design that integrates with the historic Zona Cultural district, including a brick podium and residential levels that complement adjacent historic structures. The project offers a range of apartment types and extensive amenities, with leasing and initial move-ins now in progress.

data_points.address: 110 S. Laredo St., San Antonio, TX
data_points.notable_features: 16-story building located between the historic Continental Hotel, Arana Building, and De La Garza House; features a brick podium, residential levels with brick and stucco, and units overlooking San Pedro Creek Culture Park. Amenities include a sixth-floor clubroom, coworking space, indoor/outdoor fitness center, outdoor pool deck, and a 16th-floor sky lounge.
data_points.project_notes: Part of continued growth and redevelopment on downtown San Antonio’s West Side, within the Zona Cultural district.

articles.title: Editor’s note: A verdict in lights
articles.narrative: This editor's note introduces a series of articles, highlighting a former Vornado Realty Trust leasing broker who engaged in a scheme to defraud his employer by creating a fake tenant broker and company to collect commissions on a Times Square LED billboard deal. The note also previews features on Compass's post-merger executive changes, California political races with real estate implications, and market trends in Florida, Texas, California, New York, and Illinois.

articles.title: Inside the billboard heist at Vornado
articles.narrative: A former Vornado Realty Trust executive, Jared Solomon, was convicted of stealing nearly $10 million from the company over more than a decade by creating phony brokerages and billing Vornado for leasing commissions on deals where no external broker was involved. Solomon exploited his position as a vice president in the leasing office to identify deals without brokers, then inserted his shell companies, such as Cobalt Advisors and Margoux Media, into the transactions. He falsified documents, forged signatures, and impersonated fictitious executives to maintain the elaborate scheme, which funded a lavish lifestyle. The fraud was uncovered after a lengthy investigation, leading to his conviction and a potential prison sentence of up to 82 years.

articles.title: Who’s guiding Compass’ new era
articles.narrative: Compass has appointed Neda Navab as its new president, tasking her with guiding the company into a new era following its significant $1.6 billion acquisition of Anywhere Real Estate. Navab, who previously served as chief of staff to CEO Robert Reffkin, is undertaking a 100-day listening tour to engage with agents across the country. This leadership transition and strategic integration come as Compass navigates the complexities of merging with Anywhere, which includes integrating thousands of new agents, franchise businesses, and a substantial debt load, while also working to unify corporate culture and technology platforms.

data_points.project_notes: Compass acquired Anywhere Real Estate, which included brands like Sotheby's International Realty, Corcoran, and Coldwell Banker, along with $3 billion in debt from Anywhere's balance sheets.

articles.title: Developers piece together opportunity in NYC’s rezeoned Garment District
articles.narrative: A partnership between Marty Burger and Andrew Heiberger acquired a 12-story office building at 29 West 35th Street in New York City's Garment District for $25 million. The developers plan to convert the property into a 107-unit apartment complex, with 27 units designated as affordable. The acquisition and planned conversion were contingent on the Midtown South rezoning, which passed in August 2025, allowing for increased residential density in the area. The project is also leveraging the 467m tax break, which is crucial for the financial viability of office-to-residential conversions in the current market.

data_points.address: 29 West 35th Street, New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: Planned conversion to 107 apartment units, including 27 affordable units, with a rooftop movie screen.
data_points.project_notes: The property was acquired in October 2025 after the Midtown South rezoning passed in August 2025. The project will utilize the 467m tax break for office-to-residential conversions. The site was previously acquired by Starwood Property Trust in a $23 million credit bid at a foreclosure auction in early 2022.

articles.market_intelligence: The Midtown South rezoning, passed in August 2025, transformed 42 blocks in central Manhattan to allow for 10,000 units of housing, increasing floor-area ratios from 12 to up to 18. However, developers face challenges due to construction costs, tight margins, and higher wage requirements under the 485x state tax program for multifamily projects over 100 units. The 467m tax break for office-to-residential conversions is seen as a critical incentive. Asking rents for dated office buildings in the Garment District are around $60.49 per square foot, half the cost of trophy offices in Hudson Yards.

articles.title: The Closing: Jonathan Landau
articles.narrative: Jonathan Landau, founder of Landau Properties, recently secured $213 million in financing for a planned 47-story residential tower at 205 Montague Street in Brooklyn. This project marks a significant step for his new firm, which he co-founded with his daughter, son, and son-in-law after departing Fortis in 2022. Landau aims to develop properties that are "a little bit better" than competitors, drawing on his experience with successful projects like Olympia Dumbo, a 33-story condo tower that set Brooklyn sales records. The Montague Street development is strategically located at the intersection of Downtown Brooklyn and Brooklyn Heights, an area Landau considers prime for development.

data_points.notable_features: Planned 47-story residential tower on the eastern edge of Brooklyn Heights.

articles.title: Inside Naples’ ultra-luxury shift
articles.narrative: Naples, FL, traditionally a seasonal market, is rapidly transforming into a year-round ultra-luxury hub, attracting affluent transplants who are spending more time and money in the area. This shift is evidenced by record-breaking residential sales, including a $225 million beachfront estate and an $85 million waterfront mansion in the exclusive Port Royal neighborhood. The market's appeal is further bolstered by new luxury amenities, a rebuilding of the Port Royal private club, and a strong pipeline of high-end condo projects, despite broader market corrections and the impact of Hurricane Ian.

companies_people.label: BUYER
companies_people.firm_name: Sculptor Diversified Real Estate Income Trust

articles.market_intelligence: The ultra-luxury market in Naples is experiencing unprecedented 'buzz' in areas like Port Royal. The overall housing market in Naples saw the largest drop in prices in the country in 2025, down 6.5 percent, but the $20 million-and-up segment is performing strongly. Pending sales in Naples were up 56 percent year-over-year in February 2026, while inventory was down 15 percent in the same period. The JW Marriott Marco Island Beach Resort and two Naples golf courses were under contract for $835 million in March 2026, indicating strong confidence in local tourism.

articles.title: How New Yorkers Erik Rutter and David Weitz became Miami’s newest up-and-comers
articles.narrative: Oak Row Equities, led by Erik Rutter and David Weitz, acquired a 4.25-acre bayfront development site in Miami's Brickell district for a record $520 million. The firm partnered with OKO Group to develop a luxury hotel and branded condominium project on the site. This acquisition marks a significant move for the relatively young firm, which has rapidly expanded its presence in Miami's prime development areas since its inception eight years ago, backed by substantial family capital.

data_points.notable_features: Bayfront site allowing for multiple supertall developments.
data_points.project_notes: Site was acquired in December 2024 after going into contract in late 2024.

articles.title: The Weekly Dirt: Formula 1 descends on Miami
articles.narrative: This article is a weekly roundup of real estate news in South Florida, focusing on the intersection of Formula 1 racing and luxury real estate. It highlights several high-profile residential condo purchases by F1 drivers in Miami, including a unit at St. Regis Residences, Miami, and a $4.9 million condo at Jean-Georges Miami Tropic Residences. The piece also covers how developers and brokers leverage major events like the Miami Grand Prix for marketing and sales, and includes a commercial office transaction in Boca Raton.

articles.title: Hidden costs besiege Chicago’s Frank Lloyd Wright homes
articles.narrative: The article highlights the unique and often challenging market for Frank Lloyd Wright-designed homes in the Chicago area, where well-maintained properties command premium prices while those with deferred maintenance face significant restoration costs. The Hills-DeCaro House in Oak Park recently sold for $2.3 million, exceeding its asking price, demonstrating strong demand for turnkey historic homes. In contrast, the J.J. Walser Jr. House in Chicago's Austin neighborhood, now owned by Fannie Mae after foreclosure, is valued at only $65,000 due to severe disrepair, with restoration costs estimated at over $2 million. This disparity underscores the high cost of preserving Wright's experimental architecture, which often features structural quirks and non-standard materials, making repairs complex and expensive.

data_points.notable_features: Frank Lloyd Wright-designed home, well-maintained, expansive yard, located among a cluster of Wright homes.
data_points.project_notes: Sold for $5,000 above asking price; went under contract within 48 hours of being teased in Crain's, before private listing.

people.name: Safina Uberoi
people.title: Board member, Frank Lloyd Wright Conservancy

articles.market_intelligence: The market for historic homes, especially Frank Lloyd Wright-designed properties, has 'exploded' in recent years. Well-maintained Wright homes command a premium, while those with deferred maintenance can require restoration costs double or more than the purchase price. The Wright-designed Winslow House in River Forest sold for $2.2 million in 2024. The J.J. Walser Jr. House in Chicago's Austin neighborhood is valued at $65,000, with an estimated $2 million needed for restoration. Wright's homes are known for structural quirks, leaky roofs, and unorthodox construction methods, making repairs difficult and expensive.

articles.title: Texas officials’ legal campaign against Muslim-centric project decried as “Islamophic witch hunts”
articles.narrative: Texas state officials, including Governor Greg Abbott and Attorney General Ken Paxton, are engaged in a protracted legal battle to block The Meadow, a Muslim-centric community development in North Texas. The developer, Community Capital Partners, recently secured a win in Travis County, compelling the Texas Workforce Commission to review updated housing policies for the project. However, Paxton plans to appeal this ruling and continues to pursue two additional lawsuits against the developer, alleging securities law violations and evasion of state oversight by the project's municipal utility district. Critics have condemned the state's actions as "Islamophobic witch hunts" and "politically driven regulatory harassment."

articles.title: After California: Scott Wiener, housing policy wonk, aims for Congress
articles.narrative: This article profiles California State Senator Scott Wiener, a prominent figure in the YIMBY (Yes, in my Backyard) housing movement, as he campaigns for a congressional seat in San Francisco. The piece highlights Wiener's legislative achievements in California, which include upzoning properties along transit lines, streamlining environmental reviews for development, eliminating single-family zoning, and strengthening housing production mandates. Wiener's efforts have significantly influenced housing policy, spreading the YIMBY ideology nationwide. The article also discusses the challenges he faces in a more divided Congress and the broader limitations of legislation in addressing the housing crisis, noting that despite policy changes, new housing remains scarce and expensive in California.

articles.title: The real estate industry picks its candidate in CA-11
articles.narrative: This article examines the significant financial contributions from the real estate industry to candidates in San Francisco's competitive CA-11 congressional race. State Sen. Scott Wiener has garnered over $123,000 from real estate professionals, representing nearly 5% of his total individual contributions, reflecting the industry's support for his pro-housing stance. In contrast, Supervisor Connie Chan, who has opposed state housing mandates, received $4,000 from real estate donors, while tech centimillionaire Saikat Chakrabarti, largely self-financing his campaign, received approximately $3,200 from the industry. The article also notes the resurgence of San Francisco's office market, driven by the artificial intelligence sector, with office demand surpassing pre-pandemic levels.
articles.market_intelligence: Office demand in San Francisco surpassed its 2018-2019 average through March, making it the only key U.S. market tracked by VTS to do so. New York City's office demand exceeded pre-pandemic levels at various points but fell short in Q1 this year. Other cities like Boston, Chicago, Washington, D.C., Seattle, and Los Angeles have not seen full recovery in demand. The AI industry is a primary driver, with 67 new AI companies touring offices in San Francisco between January and March.

articles.title: Wall Street Celebrates Big Tech's AI Growth, But Concerns Bubble Under The Surface
articles.narrative: Wall Street celebrated strong first-quarter earnings from major tech companies, driven by significant investments in artificial intelligence. Microsoft, Google, Amazon, and Meta reported higher-than-expected revenue growth, with cloud services and generative AI products showing substantial gains. This performance is seen by some as validation for the unprecedented data center spending spree. However, other analysts caution that rising costs for AI infrastructure, including electricity, equipment, and labor, are creating headwinds. Additionally, concerns are emerging about the concentration of cloud revenue from a small number of AI model-making customers, particularly Microsoft's reliance on OpenAI, which some view as a significant counterparty risk.
articles.market_intelligence: The overall cloud market grew by 35% year-over-year in Q1 2026, marking the industry's highest quarterly growth rate since 2021. In 2021, the cloud market was 40% of its current size. The cost of building and leasing new data centers is increasing due to supply constraints affecting prices for electricity, critical equipment, optical fiber, and labor. Project timelines are also prolonged by rising community opposition and legal hurdles. Shortages of graphics processing units (GPUs) and other AI-specific chips and IT gear are driving up equipment costs.

articles.title: Tenants Gain Even More Power In Bay Area Lab Market
articles.narrative: The Bay Area's life sciences real estate market experienced a challenging first quarter, with negative net absorption of 453,000 SF and availability rising to 32.3%. Sublease space remains abundant, particularly in South San Francisco, leading to aggressive pricing by sublandlords. Despite a 9% year-over-year decline in asking rents to $5.57 per SF and a marketwide vacancy rate of 29%, positive indicators in the broader biotech industry suggest a potential rebound in leasing activity by late 2026 or early 2027. Landlords are focusing on occupancy to generate cash flow, while tenants are leveraging the market conditions to secure favorable terms, including significant concessions and tenant improvement allowances.
articles.market_intelligence: Net absorption of lab space in the Bay Area turned negative in Q1 2026, with 453,000 SF of occupancy losses. Availability increased to 32.3%. Sublease space in South San Francisco totaled 630,000 SF in Q1, with 2.1M SF of sublease space available throughout the Bay Area. Base asking rents declined by 9% year-over-year to $5.57 per SF in Q1. Marketwide life sciences vacancy ticked up to 29% in Q1, from 28% in Q1 2025. The construction pipeline for labs in the Bay Area remains at zero. Approximately 14.1M SF of lab space is available in the Bay Area, including 2M SF of spec lab space delivered between 2022 and 2025.
